title: MCP Tools and How It Changed My Life
excerpt: When I first got to get my hands dirty with MCP during my internship, I didn't think it would matter much. What followed quietly changed how I work, think, and spend my time.
content:
When I first got to get my hands dirty with MCP (Model Context Protocol) during my internship, it was still a relatively new tool. The curiosity was there on me, and I liked the idea too of it, but I genuinely did not think it would matter much to my day-to-day life. I figured it was one of those technologies you learn about, nod at, and move on from.

I was wrong.

The Tab Problem I Didn't Know I Had

Before MCP, my workflow looked like most developers' workflows: a sprawling mess of browser tabs. Docs here, a Slack thread I needed to reference, a half-read email I told myself I'd get back to. At some point I stopped counting tabs and just accepted the chaos as the cost of doing work.

MCP changed that quietly. When your tools can talk to each other, when your Claude can reach into your calendar, pull up a GitHub issue, check your email for the long lost receipts of a specific item you bought from H&M, and surface exactly what you need without you having to go hunting, the tabs start closing on their own. Not because you forced a new habit, but because the friction that kept them open is just... gone. Which is quite insane for me.

No more bouncing between Gmail and Outlook hoping I hadn't missed anything. No more context-switching just to remember what was said in a meeting three days ago. The information comes to me, in context, when I need it.

It also changed how I think about building software. When tools are composable and context-aware, you stop thinking in isolated applications and start thinking in workflows. That mental shift matters because now big goals also seem to be approachable and attainable to me, and it honestly boosts my confidence.

Going Deeper

The more I used MCP, the more I wanted to understand what was happening underneath. So I started digging into the architecture, like how the protocol handles tool registration, context passing, session management. The design is genuinely elegant, and the more I read, the more I appreciated the thinking behind it.

The Honest Takeaway

I am not going to tell you MCP will change your life too. Maybe it will, maybe it won't, it depends on what you build and how you work with it. But I do see the usage of MCP in day-to-day life. I imagine people using MCP features on their AI assistants and becoming more productive, even for a normal person who has tons of emails and messages to look into.

The best tools are like that. They don't announce themselves. They just quietly make the hard parts easier until one day you look up and realize the way you work has completely changed.

If you're curious about MCP or want to talk about MCP server architecture, I'm always happy to chat.

title: The Day I "Fixed" Production
excerpt: A simple DynamoDB fix in the wrong environment taught me that owning the mistake matters more than the mistake itself.
content:
It was a simple scenario. I was working on a task where DynamoDB wasn't properly reading the data in our development environment. Instead of pausing to consult my mentor, I decided to take the "quick fix" route: I would be responsible and try to sort some things by myself.

The problem? I thought so but I wasn't just in dev. I was logged into the production AWS account.

I had the access, so I didn't think twice. It was only later, when I mentioned my "fix" to my mentor, that she cautioned me immediately that production is a different beast. Even though I was just trying to bypass a data read issue, I was operating in a space where one wrong command could have deleted entire data entry tables of my team —mission-critical data that would have caused a massive outage.

The Realization

The moment I realized the weight of what I had done, my heart dropped. A voice in my head said: Maybe nobody will notice. Maybe just stay quiet and revert it. But hiding almost always makes things worse. It creates doubt and damages trust. Speaking up for me means staying in integrity, it shows others they can rely on you even when things go wrong.

Thankfully, the data wasn't corrupted beyond repair, but it still should not have happened. Of course, after the incident, we added some guidelines and even changed the permissions granted to interns for the necessary guardrails to ensure the we as a team don't find ourselves in a similar situation again.

What I Learned

That day, I learned something important: mistakes are inevitable, and the best way to handle them is to face them. One bad moment does not define your career. A single accident does not erase all your hard work. What matters is what you do next—people remember honesty, accountability, and how quickly you learn and recover.

The Takeaway

If you never touch production, you will never break production. But you will also never grow, never lead, and never build anything important.

If you want to build, you have to be willing to accept the times you break. You aren't the first person to make a mistake in the cloud, and you won't be the last. The key is to own it, fix what you can, and take that one small lesson forward.
